title: Number Processing Constraints and Earnings News
abstract: Neuroscience shows that human brains are neurologically constrained to process small numbers linearly and large numbers logarithmically, leading to underreactions to larger numbers as their perceived difference becomes smaller. We test this hypothesis in the context of earnings announcements and find that investors respond less in the short term to earnings news for stocks with high earnings per share magnitudes, exacerbating postearnings announcement drift for these stocks. These findings are distinct from and incremental to several risk-based and behavioral explanations, attenuated by robot presence and present in a quasi-experimental design using stock splits. Our evidence suggests that number processing constraints have implications for stock price efficiency.

article.1. Introduction: Research in neuroscience suggests that numerical competence in humans is the result of various neural constraints that shape the way we perceive numbers (Dehaene 2011,Nieder 2019). A key finding in this literature is that people understand numbers on a mental number line, and this line compresses as numbers get larger (Dehaene and Mehler 1992,Longo and Lourenco 2007). To a rough approximation, the human brain processes small numbers linearly but large numbers logarithmically (Dehaene et al. 2008,Hyde and Spelke 2009). This logarithmic processing results in a slow response and general underreaction to large numbers as their perceived difference appears to be smaller than it is in reality.1Given these findings from laboratory settings, we investigate the implications of these neural constraints in the large-scale context of financial markets in which participants are constantly presented with decisions based on numbers. In this paper, we investigate this neurological phenomenon by using the stock market response to earnings news as a testing ground for what we call the number processing constraints hypothesis. This hypothesis asserts that investors respond less to the same earnings news for stocks with high earnings per share (EPS) magnitudes than they do for stocks with low EPS magnitudes.2Earnings announcements (EAs) provide an attractive setting to test our hypothesis for several reasons. First, EAs are arguably one of the most prominent corporate events, making them an economically significant setting for analyzing large-scale implications of the neural constraints in the stock market. Second, the key ingredients to test the number processing constraints hypothesis are observable. When interpreting earnings news, investors must compare two numbers of similar magnitudes—reported earnings and the analysts’ consensus forecast—which are almost always presented side by side in the media (seeFigure 1for examples). Third, to test our hypothesis, it is important to fix the information content of earnings news to ensure a proper comparison of stock market reactions to EA news based on the nominal EPS values at which the news are presented.3Unlike other settings, the EA setting benefits from a long-standing literature that has developed a consensus measure to capture the information content of EA news: standardized earnings surprise (Christie 1987,Ohlson 1991,Skinner and Sloan 2002,Livnat and Mendenhall 2006,Brown et al. 2009). Finally, this setting allows us to investigate whether a contributing explanation for the long-standing but still puzzling phenomenon of systematic underreaction to earning news (Ball and Brown 1968;Bernard and Thomas 1989,1990) is deeply rooted in the biological functioning of the human brain. To develop intuition for our prediction, we consider a simple Gordon growth pricing model in which stock price is equal to the discounted sum of expected future earnings (seeAppendix A).4The model suggests that the (rational) market reaction to EA news is increasing in the standardized earning surprise, which is the difference between actual earnings and expected earnings scaled by the preannouncement stock price. However, because number processing constraints cause investors to perceive smaller differences between actual and expected earnings as the magnitude of earnings news gets larger, the model predicts that the return response to earnings news will also be smaller. We test the number processing constraints hypothesis using the earnings response coefficient (ERC) framework with one modification: we interact measures of unexpected earnings with the absolute level of EPS. Our specification includes year–quarter and stock fixed effects to control for time trends and stock-level heterogeneity that may be related to ERCs. To address risk-based explanations for our findings, we also control for several known risk factors, including size, book to market, return on assets, leverage, growth, investment, momentum, the fraction of institutional ownership, analyst coverage, illiquidity, and the preannouncement stock price level. We find that moving from the bottom to the top decile of absolute EPS is associated with a 12.7% reduction in the market response to unexpected earnings, consistent with the number processing constraints hypothesis. Moreover, plotting the ERC by each decile of EPS magnitude, we find a decreasing and concave relationship between ERC and EPS magnitude. Our main findings are robust to a battery of robustness tests. First, the results are not sensitive to excluding large raw (unscaled) earnings surprises and large EPS magnitudes or to using continuous measures of EPS magnitudes or earnings surprises. Second, prior research suggests that the level of a stock’s price can affect market reaction to earnings news (Cheong and Thomas 2018,Shue and Townsend 2021). Our evidence suggests that the magnitude of EPS matters even after controlling for various functional forms of the price effect. Third, to account for the nonlinear S-shaped relationship between earnings surprises and the market response to earnings news (e.g.,Freeman and Tse 1992,Subramanyam 1996,Skinner and Sloan 2002), we plot our results using local polynomial fits on continuous measures of both variables. We find that low-magnitude EPS stocks elicit a stronger market response at any level of EA news. This suggests that our results are not confounded by the nonlinear relationship in which large earnings surprises tend to have smaller ERCs. Instead, they highlight a source of heterogeneity in this relationship: the S-shaped relation varies with EPS magnitude. Finally, our results are distinct from limited attention as an explanation for the underreaction to earnings news. Our estimates are quantitatively similar when controlling for two commonly used proxies of limited attention: the degree of concurrent EAs and Friday EAs. Our hypothesis relies on the premise that number processing constraints affect humans, so the presence of nonhuman market participants should attenuate our estimates. Following this logic, we expect the effect of the neural constraints to be smaller for stocks with a high level of machine presence. To proxy for machine presence, we focus on cross-sectional variation in investor sophistication and Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval (EDGAR) downloads initiated by robots (Drake et al. 2015,Loughran and McDonald 2017,Ryans 2017). We find that the effect of number processing constraints is indeed less pronounced for stocks with a high presence of machines, supporting the premise that our findings are driven by a uniquely human characteristic. Number processing constraints imply that the differential market response to earnings should dissipate over time as two identical stocks with the same earnings news should ultimately converge to the same efficient price in an efficient market (Bernard and Thomas 1989,1990;Fama 1998). We, therefore, predict that stocks with high EPS magnitudes will have higher post-EA drift (PEAD) than stocks with low EPS magnitudes as they experience a greater initial underreaction to news. Using subsequent price reactions in the following 60 days, we test and find evidence consistent with this prediction. The PEAD effect increases by as much as 32.2% going from the bottom to the top decile of absolute EPS magnitudes, consistent with the interpretation that number processing constraints generate a systematic underreaction to earnings news. We further investigate the causal interpretation of our main results by employing a difference-in-differences (DiD) design based on events that significantly increase the number of outstanding shares for a given stock (and, hence, its EPS magnitude) but are plausibly exogenous to unexpected earnings: stock splits (Baker et al. 2009,Birru and Wang 2016). Whereas stock splits are a choice, prior work argues that they still provide a useful, clean, quasi-natural experiment for the following reasons. First, stock splits are cosmetic changes that contain no information about future profitability, and firm fundamentals are unlikely to change significantly around the split event (Lakonishok and Lev 1987,Asquith et al. 1989,Green and Hwang 2009). Second, it is unlikely that stock splits are related to future unexpected earnings (i.e., relative to analysts’ consensus forecasts for future earnings) because it would require managers to correctly predict positive unexpected earnings several quarters ahead to take advantage of number processing constraints. Our difference-in-differences estimates suggest that the market response to the same earnings surprise increases as much as 13% following a stock split. Likewise, PEAD drops by a similar amount following a split. We investigate a dynamic test of the parallel trends assumption to assess the internal validity of the design and to examine whether the effect is persistent. The dynamic graphs show no signs of pretrends, suggesting that confounding factors, such as selection into stock splits and anticipation, are unlikely to bias our results. Further, we find that the number processing constraints effect appears to persist over time.5Overall, these results offer further evidence supporting a causal interpretation of the number processing constraints hypothesis on the initial market response to earnings news and subsequent drift. Finally, we investigate if the dollar presentation of earnings values affects predictions of the number processing constraints hypothesis. Because conventional discussions around earnings news are almost always in terms of cents per share and quarterly EPS rarely have magnitudes greater than 100 cents,6it is likely that most investors interpret quarterly earnings news in cents per share. However, if investors interpret earnings news in dollars instead, large EPS values such as 102 may appear “smaller” at 1.02, which should lead to a sharp change in the reaction to EA news greater than 100 cents. Using a regression discontinuity test around the 100 cents threshold, we do not find any statistically significant changes in market reactions to earnings news around the 100 cents threshold. Thus, the results are consistent with the notion that investors likely interpret EA news in cents per share and support the interpretation of our main results that behave in accordance with the number processing constraints hypothesis. Our study contributes to the growing literature at the intersection of accounting, finance, and neuroscience (Dickhaut 2009). This literature uses administrative data and laboratory methods to link cognitive ability to trading performance (Grinblatt et al. 2011,2012;Ackert et al. 2018), explain asymmetric responses to gains and losses (Knutson et al. 2009,Barton et al. 2014,Kuhnen 2015), and relate emotional affect and visual awareness to financial decision making (Kuhnen and Knutson 2011,Farrell et al. 2014,Chen et al. 2016). Recent archival studies have linked facial structure to achievement (He et al. 2019), financial misreporting (Jia et al. 2014), and auditor tenure (Hsieh et al. 2020). Using a large sample of observational data and a quasi-natural experimental design, our study contributes by linking neuroscience to a well-known phenomenon in the stock market: the systematic underreaction to earnings news (Ball and Brown 1968,Foster et al. 1984,Bernard and Thomas 1990). Whereas underreaction to and subsequent drift following major news announcements could be related to the underlying risks, a literature followsBernard and Thomas (1989)that examines explanations ranging from behavioral biases (e.g.,Daniel et al. 1998,Liang 2003,Chordia and Shivakumar 2005,Frazzini 2006,DellaVigna and Pollet 2009,Hirshleifer et al. 2009,Chi and Shanthikumar 2017,Huang et al. 2018) to limits to arbitrage (Barberis et al. 1998). We contribute to this literature by documenting evidence of a neurological phenomenon that is distinct and incremental to these other explanations. This literature shows that there is a nonlinear relationship between market reaction and earnings surprises (Subramanyam 1996,Skinner and Sloan 2002), and we extend this literature by showing evidence of heterogeneity in this nonlinear relationship based on number processing constraints. Further, our results not only show that neurological phenomena may have economic relevance for stock price efficiency, but also that the proliferation of information technology may mitigate the effects. Our paper also relates to a growing literature on information processing in financial markets (Carlin 2009,Korniotis and Kumar 2011,Birru and Wang 2016), analyst forecasts (Roger et al. 2018), and individual investor trading (Frydman et al. 2018,Frydman and Wang 2020). Among these, a related paper isShue and Townsend (2021), which shows that nonproportional thinking (seeThaler 1980,Tversky and Kahneman 1981) has important implications in that a doubling of a stock’s price is associated with substantial (20%–30%) declines in volatility, beta, and return response to stock-specific news (e.g., CEO hires, mergers and acquisitions, ratings, etc.). Our paper makes a distinct contribution in a few important ways. First,Shue and Townsend (2021)argue that, because investors think about the impact of news on price in nominal terms, nonproportional thinking should lead markets to react more strongly for lower priced stocks.7Our findings demonstrate that the level of earnings matters, not just the price basis for comparison, in the context of earnings announcements. Second, our results are robust to controlling for lagged price, indicating that not only is number processing constraints a cognitive bias that may arise independently of nonproportional thinking, but also that it is distinct empirically. Third, we show that number processing constraints offer a partial explanation for PEAD that is distinct from investor attention and suggests that our ERC findings reflect systematic underreaction. Biases in processing numbers are also documented in other related settings.Cheong and Thomas (2011)find that analyst forecast errors do not vary with the scale of EPS, whereasJung et al. (2017)show that market participants fixate on EPS in absolute dollars. The analyst literature also documents that analysts suffer from biases related to anchoring (Cen et al. 2013,Clarkson et al. 2020), first impressions (Hirshleifer et al. 2021), and decision fatigue (Hirshleifer et al. 2019). Overall, our findings suggest that the market reaction to earnings, and subsequent drift following initial reactions, is subject to number processing constraints.
article.2. Related Literature and Theoretical Fr: The first empirical evidence of market reaction to EAs and subsequent drift is documented inBall and Brown (1968)and subsequently verified and replicated by studies such asFoster et al. (1984),Bernard and Thomas (1989,1990),Hung et al. (2015), andLawrence et al. (2018). Despite extensive research in this area, earnings news remains an important research topic with many questions requiring further study. In particular, no consensus has been reached on the exact mechanism behind the existence of PEAD. Extant theoretical and empirical research suggests that these phenomena may be driven by mispricing because of investor underreaction to earnings news, investors’ inability to properly account for inflation in their reaction to earnings, and limits to arbitrage (Barberis et al. 1998,Daniel et al. 1998,Chordia and Shivakumar 2005). Several studies point toward psychological and behavioral biases as potential explanations for investor underreaction to earnings news. Limited attention is perhaps the most widely accepted explanation (e.g.,DellaVigna and Pollet 2009,Hirshleifer et al. 2009), but studies propose alternative psychological explanations. For example,Liang (2003)argues that overconfidence in private signals may drive PEAD, whereasFrazzini (2006)shows that the response patterns seem to fit the disposition effect predicted by prospect theory.Hirshleifer et al. (2008)find evidence inconsistent with individual investors contributing to PEAD.Balakrishnan et al. (2010)suggest that investors, because of limited capacity constraints, tend to use a simplified model to interpret earnings news. More recently,Chi and Shanthikumar (2017)find that investors’ local bias has a significant impact on ERC and PEAD. The salience of firm press release headlines can lead to stronger initial reactions to EAs and weaker PEAD (Huang et al. 2018). We build on this literature by studying this long-standing but still puzzling phenomenon from a new angle by asking whether it could be rooted in the biological nature of the human brain. Decades of research in neuroscience shows that humans’ ability to make sense of numbers is a product of various neural constraints, deeply linked to our neuronal heritage as primates and vertebrates (Nieder 2020). Instead of a regular number line in which numbers are equally spaced, humans process numbers on a mental number line that compresses as numbers get larger (Dehaene and Mehler 1992;Dehaene et al. 1993,1998;Hubbard et al. 2005;Longo and Lourenco 2007;Dehaene 2011). In other words, the human brain processes small numbers linearly, whereas it processes large numbers logarithmically (Dehaene et al. 2008,Hyde and Spelke 2009). This phenomenon is similar to the Weber–Fechner law, which states the sensitivity to a stimulus is inversely proportional to the size of each stimulus (e.g.,Stigler 1965;Thaler 1980,1999). Dehaene (2011)describes an experiment used in the neuroscience literature (Banks and Coleman 1981,Viarouge et al. 2010) to identify the existence of number processing constraints in various experimental subjects with different levels of cognitive ability (e.g., adults, children, aboriginals). In the experiment, subjects are shown two series of numbers and told that each was randomly generated. Subjects were then asked to quickly rank the series by how randomly and evenly the two series of numbers are distributed. The two series, distributed between 1 and 2,000 are the following: Series A: 879, 5, 1,322, 1,987, 212, 1,776, 1,561, 437, 1,098, 663 Series B: 238, 5, 689, 1,987, 16, 1,446, 1,018, 58, 421, 117 Subjects typically respond that series B is more evenly spread out than series A because large numbers in series A seem to appear too often. However, series A was generated from a uniform distribution, and series B was generated from an exponential distribution. Upon further inspection, patterns emerge that distinguish the two series: the numbers in series A are regularly spaced by around 200 units, and those in series B are less evenly spaced with fewer high numbers. Subjects tend to mistakenly select series B because it better fits with perceptions of the mental number line in which large numbers are less noticeable than small numbers. The following is a graphic illustration of the mental number line. Because of larger numbers being processed logarithmically, their perceived difference becomes smaller than it actually is. Interestingly,Dehaene et al. (2008)andHyde and Spelke (2009)find that the linear processing for smaller numbers seems to be a product of cultural intervention and education. Conducting experiments using numbers between 0 and 100 on groups of indigenous Amazonians and Americans, they find that the former’s number perception completely exhibits logarithmic patterns, whereas the latter’s exhibits a mix of linear and logarithmic patterns, suggesting that logarithmic processing is an innate characteristic that does not easily fade away. Further neuroscience experiments show that it takes significantly longer for participants to discriminate two large numerosities, such as 90 and 100, than two small ones, such as 10 and 20. To develop intuition for how number processing constraints affect market reaction to earnings news, we describe a simple model in the style of the Gordon growth pricing model (Gordon and Shapiro 1956,Gordon 1959) inAppendix A. Assuming that all stocks face a constant discount rate and earnings are fully paid out to investors, the model shows that the (rational) return response to the arrival of earnings news is increasing in the difference between actual earnings and expected earnings scaled by the pre-EA stock price.8However, because number processing constraints cause the difference between actual and expected earnings to be perceived as smaller for earnings news with large EPS magnitude, it follows that the market reaction to such earnings news will also be smaller. Based on this intuition, we propose the following number processing constraints hypothesis in the context of EA (stated in the alternative form). Investors respond less to the same earnings news for stocks with high EPS magnitudes than they do for stocks with low EPS magnitudes. In other words, given the same content of earnings news, does the EPS magnitude at which it is presented matter? There are, however, valid reasons why the market reaction to earnings news would not be associated with EPS magnitude. First, given that a stock’s nominal EPS magnitude is arbitrary because of its dependence on the number of outstanding shares, it is possible that we observe a null effect. Second, the neuroscience literature finds that the degree of logarithmic processing is task-dependent (Dehaene et al. 2008,Viarouge et al. 2010). Third, the presence of sophisticated investors may attenuate any pricing effects from investors subject to number processing constraints. Because it is difficult to predict ex ante whether the prediction from neuroscience applies in the particular task of processing earnings news, we take this hypothesis to the data.
article.3. Data: 3.1. Data and Variables We collect data from three primary sources, which includes the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP), Institutional Brokers’ Estimate System (I/B/E/S), and Compustat, spanning a period of 36 years from 1983 to 2018. First, we obtain daily stock returns data for ordinary common shares from CRSP to construct our main dependent variablesCAR[−1,1]iqandCAR[2,60]iq. The three-day market-adjusted cumulative abnormal returns (CARs),CAR[−1,1]iq, capture the market reaction to stocki’s EA of quarterq.CAR[2,60]iqis the market-adjusted cumulative abnormal returns over a period of day +2 to +60 relative to stocki’s EA of quarterq, capturing the PEAD effect. Specifically, we calculate them as follows: Second, we obtain information about EAs and analyst forecasts from I/B/E/S. Because the research question is on how investors respond to earnings per share values as they were reported, we focus on unadjusted actual earnings per share and unadjusted analyst quarterly forecasts.10We next construct our main independent variable,Decile_absEPS, which is the decile rank of the absolute value of reported EPS values. We use the decile rank of our main independent variable in our analysis to be consistent with prior literature studying the cross-sectional variation in market reactions to EAs (e.g.,Hirshleifer et al. 2009). Additionally, using decile rank helps to mitigate the skewness in the distribution ofabs(EPS)and to facilitate easy interpretation of the economic magnitude of the effects. However, as demonstrated inSections 4.1.2and4.1.3, the results are robust to usingabs(EPS)and the natural log ofabs(EPS). Following prior literature (e.g.,Christie 1987,Ohlson 1991,Skinner and Sloan 2002,Livnat and Mendenhall 2006,Brown et al. 2009), we define standardized earnings surprise as follows: Defining standardized unexpected earnings (SUE) using stock price as a deflator instead of the consensus forecast is important for two reasons. First, to examine the effects of number processing constraints, it is critical to use a measure of SUE that holds fixed the information content of EA news and allows us to assess whether the market reactions to EAs vary depending on the nominal magnitude of a stock’s EPS. InAppendix A, we discuss in detail how raw earnings surprise scaled by stock price is the most appropriate measure because it captures the information content of EA news, whereas earnings news scaled by the consensus forecast or the absolute value of the consensus forecast does not. Second, scaling by stock price avoids the econometric challenges of deflating by consensus forecasts that could be (i) negative or have the opposite sign with the actual reported earnings,12(ii) close or equal to zero or otherwise distort the content of earnings news. Neither of these reasons are new. They are the primary reasons the accounting literature identifies price as an appropriate deflator when calculating standardized earnings surprise (e.g.,Brown et al. 1987,2009;Christie 1987;Easton and Zmijewski 1989;Easton and Harris 1991;Ohlson 1991;Ohlson and Shroff 1992;Skinner and Sloan 2002;Livnat and Mendenhall 2006). Another common and accepted measure of SUE in the literature is raw earnings surprise scaled by the standard deviation of analyst forecasts (e.g.,Mendenhall 2004,Jegadeesh and Livnat 2006,Shue and Townsend 2021), so we verify in the Online Appendix that our main results are robust to measuring the content of earnings news using this alternative.13 Finally, to mitigate the possibility that our findings capture risk factors stemming from firms’ characteristics and information environments, we include additional control variables (e.g.,Doyle et al. 2006,Roger et al. 2018). In particular, we control forSizeiq(the log of stocki’s total assets in quarterq),BMiq(the book-to-market ratio of stockiin quarterq),ROAiq(the return on assets of stockiin quarterq),Leverageiq(stocki’s total liabilities divided by its total assets in quarterq),Growthiq(the percentage change in sales from quarterq− 1 to quarterqfor stocki),Investmentiq(the percentage change in total assets from quarterq− 2 to quarterq− 1 for stocki),MOMiq(stocki’s return for the 11-month period ending one month prior to the month of the EA in quarterq),IOiq, (stocki’s fraction of institutional ownership as of quarterq),Number of Analystsiq(the number of distinct analysts following stockiin quarterq),Illiquidityiq(the Amihud’s illiquidity of stockicalculated using calendar days [−365,−3] relative to the EA date). Finally, because recent work suggests that price levels may be related to market responses to EAs (e.g.,Cheong and Thomas 2018,Shue and Townsend 2021), we includeLagged Priceiq, the stock price two days before firmi’s EA of quarterq, in all regressions to ensure that our effects are not driven by potential price-level effects. We drop observations with missing values in all variables and winsorize all variables at the top and bottom 1%. The final panel sample consists of 229,944 observations. 3.2. Across all specifications, the coefficient of interest remains negative and statistically significant. 4.1.2. Results Controlling for the Price Level Effect. Recent research suggests that market reactions to earnings news can vary based on stock price levels. However, the evidence on this relationship has been mixed. For example,Shue and Townsend (2021)find that high-priced stocks have lower ERCs than low-priced stocks, a phenomenon that they refer to as nonproportional thinking.Cheong and Thomas (2018), in contrast, find that high-priced stocks have higher ERCs than low-priced stocks. In our analysis, we are agnostic about the effect of price levels on EA returns and simply include lagged price level and its interaction with earnings surprise as controls in all analysis. However, given the correlation between EPS magnitude and price level, we assess the robustness of our results to alternative functional forms for bothLagged Priceandabs(EPS). Main Results with Continuous SUE and the Nonlinear Relation Between SUE and CAR. To more directly account for the nonlinear relationship between earnings surprise and market reaction documented in the literature (e.g.,Freeman and Tse 1992,Subramanyam 1996,Skinner and Sloan 2002), we first present our main results graphically inFigure 2in which we use continuous measures of earnings surprises instead of decile ranks. We present two graphs. The left plot compares the bottom two and top two deciles of EPS magnitudes, and the right plot compares the bottom and top deciles of EPS magnitudes. Thex-axis shows values of earnings surprises (SUE), and they-axis shows market reaction to earnings news (CAR[−1,1]). We use local polynomials to fit the nonlinear relationship betweenSUEandCAR[−1,1]. Consistent with prior literature, the curves in both graphs resemble an arctan function. More importantly, comparing between low and high EPS magnitude cases, market reaction is indeed stronger when EPS magnitudes are low. The gap between the two curves at a given level of earnings news is also larger when we compare the bottom and top deciles of EPS magnitudes instead of the bottom two and top two deciles. The effect is also symmetric for both negative and positive earnings news. This evidence corroborates our main results on the number processing constraints hypothesis and suggests that our findings are not confounded by the nonlinear nature of the relationship betweenSUEandCAR[−1,1]. Instead, our results highlight a third dimension to this S-shaped relationship, which is its dependence on the magnitude of EPS. To formally investigate whether our results are sensitive to using a continuous measure of standardized earnings surprise, we repeat the analysis inTable 3usingSUEinstead of its decile rank and report the results inTable C.2. The coefficients of interaction terms betweenSUEand various functional forms ofabs(EPS)are all negative and statistically significant even after controlling for the price-level effect. These results suggest that our findings are robust to using continuous measure of earnings surprise. 4.1.4. Results Controlling for Limited Attention. Whereas the literature documents various explanations for differences in EA reactions, limited attention is widely considered a dominant explanation for the systematic underreaction to earnings news. In this section, we investigate whether number processing constraints are distinct from limited attention. Given the deeply rooted and biological nature of number processing by the human brain discussed inSection 2, we argue that investors may still suffer from these neural constraints regardless of how much attention they pay or time they spend analyzing earnings news so long as human judgment is present in the process. To address this potential alternative explanation, we first investigate whether there are significant associations between EPS magnitudes, our variable of interest, and two commonly used measures of limited attention. The first measure isNRANKiq, the (quarterly sorted) decile rank of the number of EAs occurring on the same day of firmi’s EAs in quarterqin the spirit ofHirshleifer et al. (2009). The intuition behind this measure is that investors are more likely to be distracted when there are many EAs occurring on the same day. The second measure isFridayiq, an indicator for whether firmi’s quarterqEA is on a Friday.DellaVigna and Pollet (2009)show evidence consistent with investor attention being lower on Fridays. Figure 3graphically represents the results. The relationships with confidence intervals are drawn using fitted local polynomial functions, and each circle represents one cent per share. Across all three panels, the relationships betweenabs(EPS)and the attention measures are relatively flat, suggesting that there is unlikely a systematic correlation between EPS magnitudes and market attention. To formally investigate whether number processing constraints are distinct from limited attention, we reestimateEquation (1), controlling for measures of limited attention as well as their interaction withDecile_SUE. The regression results are presented inTable 4. First, the coefficients on the interaction terms betweenDecile_SUEand measures of limited attention are negative and statistically significant. This is consistent with prior literature that shows low investor attention is associated with low market reactions. Second, across all specifications, the coefficient onDecile_SUE×Decile_absEPSremains negative and statistically significant. Furthermore, after controlling for limited attention, the economic magnitudes are quantitatively similar to those reported inTable 2. Mechanisms: Investor Sophistication and Robot Presence In this section, we conduct two sets of tests investigating the mechanism underlying the number processing constraints to further support our main inferences and to narrow down the scope of potential alternative explanations. If number processing constraints are a unique neurological limitation of humans, then we expect the effect to be attenuated when machines are present. Following this logic, we focus on two sources of cross-sectional variation: investor sophistication and robot presence. First, we argue that sophisticated institutional investors likely have access to advanced technology and models that would help them process the information content of earnings news. To the extent that such technological resources mitigate the effects of neurological biases, we expect that the underreaction to earnings news because of number processing constraints is smaller for stocks with a high level of institutional ownership. To test this prediction, we interactDecile_SUE×Decile_absEPSwithHigh IOas an indicator variable equal to one if a firm’s fraction of institutional ownership is in the top quartile and zero otherwise. The coefficient of this triple interaction term is expected to be positive, which would indicate that the negative effect of number processing constraints is attenuated for stocks with a high level of institutional ownership. The results are reported in column (1) ofTable 5. The coefficient of the triple interaction term is positive and statistically significant across all columns, which is consistent with our prediction. Number Processing Constraints and Post-EA Drift In this section, we investigate the implications of number processing constraints for stock returns following EAs because systematic evidence of subsequent drift supports the interpretation of the initially small market response for high EPS magnitude stocks as underreaction. Specifically, we study the effect of EPS magnitudes on PEAD. PEAD refers to the phenomenon in which stock returns continue to drift in the direction of earnings surprise for a long period after the EAs. Its persistence makes it a well-known and robust challenge to the efficient market hypothesis (Bernard and Thomas 1989,1990;Fama 1998). Whereas there has not been a consensus on the exact mechanism behind PEAD, prior research suggests that PEAD is driven by mispricing because of systematic underreaction to earnings news.21 If PEAD is driven by underreaction, our findings from the previous sections suggest that high EPS magnitude stocks with a larger initial underreaction should see stronger PEAD afterward. On the other hand, low EPS magnitude stocks with smaller initial underreaction to earnings news should have a weaker drift subsequently. The intuition is that, if the market is efficient, two identical stocks with the same underlying news should converge in price eventually. If investors process earnings information less efficiently and underreact at the time of the stock’s EA, more information is left to be impounded into the stock’s price during the drift window. The Concavity of Logarithmic Processing: Corroborating Evidence In this section, we conduct three tests to further assess the concavity of the relationship between EPS magnitudes and investor reactions to earnings news as predicted by the number processing constraints hypothesis. First, we replace ourDecile_absEPSvariable inEquation (1)with 10 indicators for each decile ofabs(EPS)(the 10th decile is normalized as a benchmark) and rerun the regression. The results are reported graphically inFigure 4. The graphs plot the ERC for each decile ofabs(EPS)relative to the 10th decile. We also use a local polynomial fit to capture the nonlinear relationship betweenabs(EPS)and the ERC. The figure suggests a decreasing and concave relationship betweenabs(EPS)and the ERC, consistent with the notion that larger numbers are processed more logarithmically. Second, if logarithmic thinking is the driving force behind our results, we would expect the effect to be symmetric around positive and negative EPS values. To assess this conjecture, we rerunEquation (2)on a subsample with positive reported EPS and a subsample with negative reported EPS. As shown inTable C.4, we do not find evidence suggesting that there is a statistically significant asymmetric effect for positive versus negative EPS values. Finally, we examine if EPS magnitudes are associated with stock volatility. If stocks with high EPS magnitudes experience lower return responses because large numbers are perceived as closer to each other, then we would expect stock volatility to be lower for these stocks. As shown inTable C.6, there is a negative association between EPS magnitude and stock volatility, consistent with the number processing constraints hypothesis. These results are also consistent with the findings inShue and Townsend (2021)that higher priced stocks have lower volatility.
article.5. Stock Split Analysis: When drawing inferences from the previous section, a potential concern may be that EPS magnitudes are endogenous to the market response to earnings news. Specifically, there could be selection on unobservables in which different groups of investors select to trade large and small EPS stocks for reasons other than the level of EPS. That same unobserved characteristic may also influence the market reaction to earnings news. Although these identification concerns are possible, they are unlikely. For a confounding factor to explain our results, it must also cause investors to underreact more to earnings news of high EPS magnitude stocks and vice versa. In our analysis, we also include controls for various time-varying observed characteristics and stock fixed effects for unobserved time-invariant stock characteristics, which help restrict alternative explanations based on the extent to which investor clienteles screen on these stock characteristics. Nevertheless, to better account for any potential omitted factors, we follow prior literature and focus on a setting that provides identifying variation in EPS magnitudes that is plausibly exogenous to the market response to unexpected earnings: stock splits (e.g.,Baker et al. 2009,Birru and Wang 2016).24Although stock splits are part of the firm’s action set and, therefore, not randomly assigned by the board of directors, we argue that stock splits provide a relatively clean quasi-natural experiment in this context for two reasons. First, prior evidence suggests that stock splits are simply a cosmetic change to reduce prices to a “normal” trading range and do not contain information about future profitability (e.g.,Lakonishok and Lev 1987,Asquith et al. 1989,Birru and Wang 2016). Furthermore, firm fundamentals are unlikely to change significantly from before to after the split events (Baker et al. 2009,Green and Hwang 2009). Therefore, any changes to market reactions to earnings news are unlikely driven by the mere act of doing stock splits. Second, we argue that managers do not make stock split decisions to take advantage of number processing constraints because such decisions require them to accurately anticipate positive unexpected earnings several quarters ahead. Because the effect of number processing constraints applies to both positive and negative earnings surprises and stock splits have a lasting effect on EPS magnitude, the number processing constraints of investors could work against managers in future periods if they are unable to beat expectations. Therefore, there is a cost to commit to a lower absolute EPS magnitude as a result of splitting. Because of this commitment cost and the fact that managers cannot perfectly foresee beating expectations in future quarters, it is unlikely that stock splits are implemented strategically to exploit number processing constraints. In other words, stock splits are likely unrelated to future unexpected earnings. These reasons allow us to more credibly attribute changes in market reactions following stock splits to changes in EPS magnitudes.25We begin by identifying all stock split events from the CRSP database over the sample period from 1983 to 2018. Next, for each stock split event of a stock, we identify the first fiscal quarter whose EPS value reported in the stock’s EA is affected. To do so, we denote a fiscal quarter for a stock as an “event quarter” if there is a stock split occurring two days before the EA of that quarter and two days after the EA of the immediately previous quarter. This is because, if changes in the number of common shares outstanding from stock dividends or stock splits occur after the fiscal period end but before the financial statements are issued or are available to be issued, the per-share calculations of EPS for that fiscal period end and any prior periods must be adjusted retrospectively based on the new number of shares.26The two-day requirement is imposed to avoid interfering with our three-day measure of market reactions. It ensures that there is no significant overlapping between the firm’s stock split event and its actual EA. This process yields a total of 6,242 stock split events. After identifying the stock split events, we construct a sample (the stock split sample hereafter) to employ a generalized DiD research design. The design is generalized because different firms have stock splits at different points in time. For each stock–event quarter, we consider six quarters before the event quarter in which a stock split becomes effective to six quarters after the event quarter. The generalized difference-in-differences design allows us to have an implicit control group of stocks. Specifically, stocks having stock splits later serve as control stocks for those that have stock splits early (i.e., the treated stocks).27 Panel B ofTable 1provides descriptive statistics of variables used in the stock split sample. The mean and median ofCAR[−1,1]are 0.726% and 0.497%, respectively. The mean and median ofCAR[2,60]are 1.666% and 1.138%, respectively. ForSUE, the mean and median are 0.000018 and 0.0003. The average magnitude of EPS is 54.72 cents, whereas the median magnitude is 43 cents. These values are similar to those reported in the main sample. 5.1. This step is important as there are two components in the calculation of EPS: net income and the number of outstanding common shares. Parallel Trends In this section, we present dynamic tests of the parallel trends assumption to support stock splits as a plausible source of exogenous variation in EPS magnitudes. A violation of the parallel trends test might suggest that managers anticipated the effect of stock splits on market reactions or intentionally selected into doing stock splits as a result. If this were the case, the posttreatment outcomes for the control group would not be an appropriate counterfactual for the posttreatment outcomes for the treated group. Decomposing thePosti,tvariable into a series of time indicators, we estimate the following dynamic treatment effect regression equations: Panel (a) ofFigure 5offers a graphic representation ofEquation (6)for the four quarters before and after the split. Thex-axis shows the quarter relative to the event quarter of a stock. They-axis shows the differential changes in the earnings response coefficient (i.e., the coefficient estimates for theβss). The figure shows no evidence of pretrends, suggesting that the parallel trends assumption is unlikely violated. Additionally, the stock split effect is persistent over time and fluctuates around 10%, similar to those reported inSection 5.2. Panel (b) ofFigure 5shows the dynamic graphs for the PEAD effects based onEquation (7). Likewise, we find no evidence of pretrends, and the effect is persistent over time. Together, these findings support the validity of stock splits as a quasi-natural experiment to assess the effect of EPS magnitudes on market reaction to earnings news and subsequent drift. A potential concern with stock splits is that they may alter the shareholder base, which could lead to a change in investor attention postsplit. However, it is unclear whether such a change would lead to an increase or decrease in attention because the potential new shareholders may be more or less attentive than the old shareholders. Additionally, the persistence of the effect shown inFigure 5alleviates this concern because a potential increase in attention around a stock split event is likely to be transitory. Nevertheless, to further address the possibility that stock splits may have a persistent impact on investor attention, we repeat our stock-split analysis, controlling for the aggregate daily trading volume scaled by the number of shares outstanding in a quarter as a proxy for investor attention. The results reported inTable C.8suggest that any changes in attention captured by trading volume around stock splits do not explain our findings. This is consistent with our panel regressions results, which do not rely on stock splits. Additionally, similar to the panel regression results, we find in panel B ofTable C.6no statistically significant effect of number processing constraints onCAR[−1,60], consistent with the notion that stocks with different EPS magnitudes in a givenSUEdecile have a similar total market reaction to earnings news. Our stock-split results are also robust to controlling forCAR[−1,1], the market reaction at the EA, as shown in panel B ofTable C.7.
article.6. Additional Analysis: 6.1. Regression Discontinuity Around the 100 Cents Threshold The units in which earnings news is reported may affect the way it is perceived by investors. If investors interpret earnings news in dollars instead of cents, they may be less affected by number processing constraints because earnings values would mostly be considered small on the mental number line. However, there are reasons to believe that investors interpret quarterly earnings in cents per share. For example, in practice, common discussions regarding earnings news (e.g., beating or missing consensus forecast) are almost always in terms of cents. Similarly, quarterly earnings news is typically reported in cents per share because it is rarely greater than 100 cents per share.29Therefore, it would be natural for investors to interpret EPS values in terms of cents (e.g., $1.07 is interpreted as 107 rather than 1.07 or 1 plus some fraction) and compare values at this magnitude in accordance with the number processing constraints hypothesis. Nevertheless, in this section, we investigate more directly whether the presentation of earnings values in dollars affects the prediction from the number processing constraints hypothesis. Given that earnings news is more likely to be presented in dollar terms when it exceeds 100 cents, we first show inTable C.9that our main findings are indeed robust to excluding earnings per share values greater than 100 cents (or $1). Second, we estimate a regression discontinuity design around the 100 cents threshold to examine whether there are any abrupt changes in how investors interpret earnings news at that threshold. The intuition is that, if the dollar presentation causes large EPS values, such as 115, to appear smaller at 1.15, there would be a sharp increase in the reaction to earnings news greater than the 100 cents threshold. The regression discontinuity design is stated as follows: The results are reported inTable 10. We consider a bandwidth around the threshold, [90;110], and two polynomial fits, linear and quadratic. In columns (1) and (2), we present the results with no controls. In columns (3) and (4), we include firm controls and their interactions withDecile_SUE. Across all specifications and measures of earnings surprises, we fail to find a statistically significant discontinuity, suggesting that there are no abrupt changes in market reactions to earnings news around the 100 cents threshold. This analysis lends support to the idea that most investors likely interpret quarterly earnings news in cents per share and behave according to the number processing constraints hypothesis. Treatment Effect Heterogeneity: Stacked Difference-in-Differences Whereas our main analysis uses cross-sectional relationships to show the effects of number processing bias, inSection 5, we employ a DiD design using stock splits to achieve better identification of the effect of interest. However, recent work in applied econometrics argues that, if the treatment effect varies over time, estimates of the average treatment effect from the standard two-way fixed effects staggered DiD regression (i.e., summarizing time-varying treatment effects with a singlePostcoefficient conditional on unit and time fixed effects) may suffer from the negative weights issue (de Chaisemartin and D’Haultfoeuille 2020,Goodman-Bacon 2021,Sun and Abraham 2021,Baker et al. 2022,Borusyak et al. 2024). This issue arises when already-treated firms serve as control firms and could affect the sign and the magnitude of the estimated treatment effect, potentially resulting in misleading inferences. Whereas this does not imply a failure of the design (Goodman-Bacon 2021), the literature cautions against making inferences solely based on a singlePostcoefficient. To address this concern, we use a stacked DiD design similar to that inDeshpande and Li (2019)andCengiz et al. (2019), whichBaker et al. (2022)show via simulations to be unbiased. There are three important steps in implementing this design. First, we use a short estimating time window around each stock split event to alleviate the concern that the treatment effect varies over time (which we have also done in the main analysis by considering six quarters around a stock split event). Second, we create event–quarter–specific data sets consisting of treated stocks (i.e., stocks that split in the current quarter) and clean control stocks (i.e., stocks that never split during the sample period or stocks whose first split is more than five years from the current quarter).30By construction, comparisons between stocks within each of these data sets do not involve already-treated stocks serving as control stocks. Finally, we append (stack) all such data sets into one large sample and run the following regressions: The results are reported inTable 11. In panel A, we use both stocks that never split during the sample period and stocks whose first split is more than five years from the current quarter as control firms. In panel B, we only use never-split stocks as control firms. In the first column of either panel, the coefficient is positive and statistically significant when the dependent variable isCAR[−1,1], consistent with our prediction that market reaction to earnings is stronger after a stock split. The economic magnitudes of the effect on treated stocks are similar to those reported inSection 5.2. Similarly, the last column of either panel shows a negative coefficient on the triple interaction term, consistent with weaker PEAD postsplit. Taken together, the results inTable 11show that our findings are robust in the stacked difference-in-differences design, suggesting that treatment effect heterogeneity is unlikely a cause for concern. article.7. Conclusion: Robust global evidence of PEAD combined with theoretical insights from market efficiency suggest that investors underreact to periodic announcements of unexpected earnings. However, recent studies find that PEAD is unrelated to a variety of financial market frictions, including transparency and transaction costs (Griffin et al. 2010,Hung et al. 2015). We propose and test an alternative explanation that is deeply rooted in the biological nature of the human brain. Neuroscience research shows that, because of neurological constraints, individuals process small numbers linearly but large numbers logarithmically, causing them to underreact to differences in large numbers. We investigate this number processing constraints hypothesis in the context of EAs in which market participants frequently have to compare two numbers of similar magnitude (reported earnings and analysts’ consensus). We uncover patterns in data that are consistent with this hypothesis: investors react up to 12.7% less to the same earnings news for the top decile EPS magnitude stocks than they do the bottom decile EPS magnitude stocks. These findings are distinct and incremental to several risk-based stories and behavioral explanations, including limited attention, and the price-level effect (Cheong and Thomas 2018,Shue and Townsend 2021). Consistent with systematic underreaction, we find that the PEAD effect is stronger for high EPS magnitude stocks. We also find that the effect is attenuated by the strong presence of machines and robots, consistent with the premise that they are not subject to the same cognitive constraints as humans. We further consider within-stock variation in EPS magnitudes based on plausibly exogenous changes determined by stock splits. We find similar results, which further support the number processing constraints hypothesis. Given the economic significance of our estimates, future research should investigate the existence and consequences of neurological constraints on the economic agents’ behaviors in other contexts, such as analyst forecasts, credit ratings, or firm voluntary disclosures.
